Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a couple hours each day, but it rarely worked out that way. I'd often end up listening to lectures while doing other things around the house, or I'd sit down intending to study and then just... drift. With ten online courses, I was constantly juggling, and nothing ever felt organized. I'd spend maybe 15 hours a week total, but it was scattered and unfocused.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, logging in every day, and other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline was breathing down my neck. Being 29 and having life responsibilities that younger students don't have meant my schedule was never really my own. Deadlines definitely drove my engagement more than any internal discipline.
Q3: Usually it was either guilt or panic that got me to log in. I'd see a notification about an upcoming deadline or remember I hadn't checked in for a few days and feel that familiar knot of anxiety. Sometimes I logged in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just clicking around without really accomplishing much. The anxiety was always there, pushing me.
Q4: For a while, yeah, checking the LMS became almost automatic—like checking my phone first thing in the morning. I'd log in during my commute or while eating breakfast, just to see what was happening. But that routine was fragile. One stressful week and the habit would break, and I'd have to rebuild it all over again. It never felt stable.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I learn best by listening. I could play them while doing other things, rewind parts I missed, and they felt more personal than reading dense text. I watched a lot of video content—probably close to 300 minutes worth. The discussion boards were less useful to me, but I tried to participate a little when I could.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on my old laptop was exhausting on my eyes. I also skipped a lot of the optional practice exercises because they felt like extra work that wouldn't directly impact my grade. I was always triaging what was absolutely necessary.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to passing the course or if it actually helped me understand something I was struggling with. If it felt like busywork or something that wouldn't show up on an exam, I'd mentally deprioritize it. I needed to feel like my limited time and energy were being spent wisely, especially juggling so many courses.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're anxious about just keeping your head above water, anything labeled 'optional' automatically goes to the bottom of the list. Required activities had this weight to them—they demanded attention. Optional stuff, even if it might have been helpful, just felt like one more thing I couldn't handle.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download audio versions of lectures when I could and listen to them on my phone during my commute or while doing chores. I also had a notebook where I'd jot down key concepts while listening—old school, I know. A lot of my studying happened away from the computer entirely because I just couldn't always be tethered to the LMS.
Q10: There were whole evenings where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes, replaying concepts in my head, or even explaining things out loud to myself to help them stick. None of that would show up anywhere in the system. I'd also sometimes study with a friend from another program who'd quiz me on material. The LMS only sees clicks, not actual learning.
Q11: Yeah, I did this constantly. I'd download a lecture, watch it once online so it registered, then replay it offline multiple times over the next few days. My internet isn't always reliable, so having things saved locally was essential. The system probably thought I only watched something once when really I'd gone through it three or four times.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety spiked, and I started feeling overwhelmed by everything. I'd log in and just stare at the screen, unable to decide what to tackle first. There were days when the sadness was so heavy I couldn't focus on anything. The CBT sessions helped eventually, but there was definitely a dark period where engaging felt impossible.
Q13: All of those things hit me at different points. The workload from ten courses was honestly insane—I don't know what I was thinking. When I was tired or confused, I'd log into the LMS but just passively scroll through things without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities, like work and family stuff, constantly pulled me away from studying.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely—worrying about bills, about whether I was wasting my time, about everything. I'd finish a lecture and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it. The LMS would show I watched it, but I wasn't really there.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made it worse—I'd think about starting something and feel this wave of dread, so I'd put it off. Then the deadline would approach and the panic would set in. It's a terrible cycle, and my 22% completion rate shows exactly how badly I managed it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd cram everything into the last possible moment, which meant the quality suffered. The LMS would probably show these spikes of activity right before due dates followed by silence. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I survived—barely.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did motivate me to engage more, at least temporarily. Positive comments made me feel like maybe I could do this. But feedback was often delayed or generic, which made it hard to stay motivated. By the time I got comments on one assignment, I was already drowning in the next crisis.
Q18: The structure of having ten courses simultaneously was just too much. Each course had its own rhythm, its own deadlines, its own expectations. I couldn't keep track of everything. A more streamlined structure with clearer priorities would have helped me focus instead of constantly feeling scattered and behind.
Q19: The video-heavy format actually worked well for my learning style, so that made engagement easier. But the sheer volume of content and the lack of clear guidance on what to prioritize made everything harder. Some courses had better organization than others, and I definitely engaged more with the well-structured ones.
Q20: That's basically me. I was logging in constantly, watching videos, clicking around—but when it came to actually submitting assignments, I just couldn't follow through. The anxiety paralyzed me. I'd start something, get overwhelmed, and abandon it. High activity doesn't mean high productivity when your mental state is working against you.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone is efficient, studies offline, and just logs in to submit polished work, their activity would look minimal but their performance would be solid. I wish I could have been that person. My experience was the opposite—lots of visible activity, very little to show for it in terms of actual submissions.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often you engage, what resources you access. But they can't show understanding, effort, or struggle. They can't see that I listened to a lecture five times offline or that I spent hours agonizing over an assignment I never submitted. The emotional and cognitive side is completely invisible.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks mechanically. For me, real engagement happened in moments when I was genuinely curious or when something clicked. Those moments were rare this semester, but they existed.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting against my own anxiety just to open my laptop some days. It can't show the sadness that made everything feel pointless or the genuine desire to succeed that kept getting buried under fear and overwhelm. My engagement was a constant internal battle that no click data could ever capture.
